Problem: Design a Coupon Management System. Required distinct concurrency angle: USAGE-LIMIT REDEMPTION RACE: a coupon with a global usage limit redeemed concurrently — under a per-coupon lock, atomically check-and-decrement remaining uses so exactly `limit` redemptions succeed and the rest get a typed LimitExhausted error. Patterns: Chain of Responsibility (validation rules: Expiry → MinCart → PerUserLimit → GlobalLimit, each a handler), Strategy (DiscountStrategy). Concurrency test: N threads redeem the last K uses through a Barrier; assert exactly K succeed, N-K get the typed error, and remaining_uses == 0.

2. CONCURRENCY TEST STRENGTH: does the test PROVE the invariant under real contention (Barrier + strict post-state: counts AND uniqueness AND typed losers AND committed-state count)? 1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Domain entities, enums, value objects, and typed errors for the coupon system.
3	
4	This file is the vocabulary of the whole design. A candidate reading it top to
5	bottom should understand every "noun" the system talks about before opening the
6	service. The interesting class here is `Coupon` — it is a small state machine
7	that guards its OWN remaining-uses count. The orchestrator never decrements the
8	count by hand; it asks the coupon to do it, so the "never go below zero" rule
9	lives in exactly one place.
10	"""
11	
12	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
13	from datetime import datetime
14	from enum import Enum
15	
16	
17	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
18	# One base error for the whole domain so callers can `except CouponError` to
19	# catch anything we throw, OR catch a specific subclass for one failure mode.
20	# 🎙️ "Every error in this system extends one base. Callers can be broad or
21	#     precise — but they never have to catch a bare Exception from my code."
22	class CouponError(Exception):
23	    """Base class for every error this system raises."""
24	
25	
26	class UnknownCoupon(CouponError):
27	    """The coupon code does not exist in the catalog."""
28	
29	
30	class CouponExpired(CouponError):
31	    """The coupon's expiry instant is in the past at redemption time."""
32	
33	
34	class CartTooSmall(CouponError):
35	    """The cart subtotal is below the coupon's minimum cart value."""
36	
37	
38	class PerUserLimitReached(CouponError):
39	    """This user has already redeemed the coupon the maximum number of times."""
40	
41	
42	class LimitExhausted(CouponError):
43	    """The coupon's global usage limit is fully consumed — no uses remain.
44	
45	    This is THE error the redemption race produces: when N callers fight over
46	    the last K uses, exactly K get a coupon and the other N-K get this.
47	    """
48	
49	
50	class CouponConfigError(CouponError):
51	    """A coupon was created with contradictory or out-of-range settings."""
52	
53	
54	# ── WHY THIS ENUM ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
55	# A coupon is one of a fixed set of discount kinds. An enum makes the kind a
56	# closed vocabulary, not a free-form string a typo could break.
57	# 🎙️ "Three discount kinds, picked once. The kind selects which strategy runs."
58	class DiscountType(Enum):
59	    PERCENT_OFF = "PERCENT_OFF"   # e.g. 20% off the cart subtotal
60	    FLAT_OFF = "FLAT_OFF"         # e.g. 500 cents off the cart subtotal
61	    BOGO = "BOGO"                 # buy-one-get-one: cheapest qualifying item free
62	
63	
64	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
65	# A line item is just data: which item, its unit price, and how many. It never
66	# changes once built, so it's frozen — Python stops any accidental mutation.
67	# 🎙️ "A cart line is a value object — item id, unit price in cents, quantity.
68	#     Frozen because a line, once in the cart we're pricing, should not mutate."
69	@dataclass(frozen=True)
70	class CartLine:
71	    item_id: str
72	    unit_price_cents: int          # integer cents — never float money
73	    quantity: int
74	
75	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
76	        # WHY: validate at the boundary. A negative price or zero quantity is a
77	        #      contradictory input — reject it the moment the value is built,
78	        #      not three layers deep where the message is useless.
79	        if self.unit_price_cents < 0:
80	            raise CouponConfigError("unit_price_cents must be >= 0")
81	        if self.quantity <= 0:
82	            raise CouponConfigError("quantity must be >= 1")
83	
84	    @property
85	    def line_total_cents(self) -> int:
86	        return self.unit_price_cents * self.quantity
87	
88	
89	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
90	# The cart is what we validate and price against. It's frozen: we evaluate a
91	# snapshot of the cart, we don't mutate the customer's live cart here.
92	# 🎙️ "The cart is a frozen snapshot — a user id plus its lines. The subtotal
93	#     is derived, never stored, so it can't drift out of sync with the lines."
94	@dataclass(frozen=True)
95	class Cart:
96	    user_id: str
97	    lines: tuple[CartLine, ...]
98	
99	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
100	        if not self.lines:
101	            # WHY: an empty cart has nothing to discount. Reject early with a
102	            #      clear, typed reason instead of returning a confusing 0.
103	            raise CouponConfigError("cart must have at least one line")
104	
105	    @property
106	    def subtotal_cents(self) -> int:
107	        # WHY: derive the subtotal from the lines every time. There's no stored
108	        #      "total" field to forget to update — single source of truth.
109	        return sum(line.line_total_cents for line in self.lines)
110	
111	
112	# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
113	# The result of a successful redemption: which coupon, how much was taken off,
114	# and the final amount. Frozen because a redemption result is a receipt — once
115	# issued, nothing should change it.
116	# 🎙️ "A redemption is a receipt: coupon code, discount applied, final total.
117	#     Frozen — a receipt is a fact, not a mutable record."
118	@dataclass(frozen=True)
119	class Redemption:
120	    redemption_id: str
121	    coupon_code: str
122	    user_id: str
123	    discount_cents: int
124	    final_cents: int
125	
126	
127	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
128	# The Coupon is the heart of the design — a small state machine over its own
129	# remaining-uses counter. It is the ONLY object allowed to change that counter,
130	# and `try_consume()` is the atomic check-and-decrement that the redemption
131	# race is all about. Putting the rule here means "never below zero" is
132	# impossible to violate by construction, no matter who calls.
133	# 🎙️ "The Coupon owns its remaining uses and the one method that spends them.
134	#     The service never touches the counter directly — it asks the coupon. So
135	#     the 'last use' rule lives in exactly one place."
136	@dataclass
137	class Coupon:
138	    code: str
139	    discount_type: DiscountType
140	    expires_at: datetime
141	    min_cart_cents: int
142	    per_user_limit: int            # max redemptions allowed per user
143	    usage_limit: int               # global cap across ALL users
144	    # value is interpreted by the discount strategy: percent (0..100) or cents
145	    value: int
146	    redeemed_count: int = 0        # how many times spent globally so far
147	    # per-user tally lives here because it's intrinsic to THIS coupon's policy
148	    _per_user_counts: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
149	
150	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
151	        # WHY: a coupon with a non-positive limit or a percent over 100 is a
152	        #      config mistake. Catch it at creation, not at the first redeem.
153	        if self.usage_limit <= 0:
154	            raise CouponConfigError("usage_limit must be >= 1")
155	        if self.per_user_limit <= 0:
156	            raise CouponConfigError("per_user_limit must be >= 1")
157	        if self.min_cart_cents < 0:
158	            raise CouponConfigError("min_cart_cents must be >= 0")
159	        if self.discount_type is DiscountType.PERCENT_OFF and not (0 < self.value <= 100):
160	            raise CouponConfigError("percent value must be in 1..100")
161	        if self.discount_type is not DiscountType.PERCENT_OFF and self.value < 0:
162	            raise CouponConfigError("flat/bogo value must be >= 0")
163	
164	    @property
165	    def remaining_uses(self) -> int:
166	        # WHY: derive remaining from the limit and the count so it can never
167	        #      disagree with `redeemed_count` — one number, two views.
168	        return self.usage_limit - self.redeemed_count
169	
170	    def user_uses(self, user_id: str) -> int:
171	        return self._per_user_counts.get(user_id, 0)
172	
173	    def try_consume(self, user_id: str) -> None:
174	        """Atomically spend one global use for this user, or raise.
175	
176	        This is the critical-section body the redemption race fights over.
177	        In the single-threaded core it's just a check-and-increment. The
178	        thread-safe service wraps the call site in a per-coupon lock so the
179	        check and the increment can't be split by another thread.
180	        """
181	        # WHY: check the GLOBAL cap first. If no uses remain, the loser of the
182	        #      race lands here and gets the typed LimitExhausted — never a
183	        #      silent failure, never a negative counter.
184	        if self.remaining_uses <= 0:
185	            raise LimitExhausted(f"coupon {self.code} has no remaining uses")
186	
187	        # WHY: then the PER-USER cap. A user who already hit their personal
188	        #      limit must not consume a global use that belongs to someone else.
189	        if self.user_uses(user_id) >= self.per_user_limit:
190	            raise PerUserLimitReached(
191	                f"user {user_id} reached per-user limit for {self.code}"
192	            )
193	
194	        # WHY: only NOW do we mutate — both counters move together. Because the
195	        #      thread-safe variant holds the per-coupon lock around this whole
196	        #      method call, no other thread can observe the half-done state.
197	        self.redeemed_count += 1
198	        self._per_user_counts[user_id] = self.user_uses(user_id) + 1
199

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Discount strategies — one class per discount kind.
3	
4	The Strategy seam. The service never says `if percent: ... elif flat: ...`. It
5	holds a map from DiscountType to a DiscountStrategy and asks the strategy to
6	compute the discount. Adding a new discount kind (say STACKABLE) is a new class
7	plus one map entry — zero changes to the redemption pipeline.
8	"""
9	
10	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
11	
12	from models import Cart, Coupon, DiscountType
13	
14	
15	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
16	# Every discount kind answers the same one question: "given this coupon and
17	# this cart, how many cents come off?" That single method is the contract.
18	# 🎙️ "Strategy interface — one method, compute_discount. The service depends on
19	#     this abstraction, never on a concrete percent-or-flat branch."
20	class DiscountStrategy(ABC):
21	    @abstractmethod
22	    def compute_discount_cents(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int:
23	        """Return the discount in cents, clamped to at most the subtotal."""
24	        raise NotImplementedError
25	
26	
27	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
28	# Percent-off: take coupon.value percent of the subtotal. Clamp so a 100%
29	# coupon never produces a discount larger than the cart (final stays >= 0).
30	class PercentOffStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
31	    def compute_discount_cents(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int:
32	        # WHY: integer math on cents — `subtotal * pct // 100` keeps money exact
33	        #      and rounds down, which favors the merchant (never over-discount).
34	        raw = cart.subtotal_cents * coupon.value // 100
35	        return min(raw, cart.subtotal_cents)
36	
37	
38	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
39	# Flat-off: a fixed number of cents off, never more than the subtotal so the
40	# customer can't end up with a negative bill.
41	class FlatOffStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
42	    def compute_discount_cents(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int:
43	        # WHY: clamp to subtotal — a 500-cent coupon on a 300-cent cart takes
44	        #      off 300, not 500. The final amount is floored at zero.
45	        return min(coupon.value, cart.subtotal_cents)
46	
47	
48	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
49	# Buy-one-get-one: the single cheapest qualifying UNIT is free. We expand lines
50	# into unit prices, and if there are two or more units, the smallest is the
51	# discount. With one unit there's no "second one" to give free, so discount = 0.
52	class BogoStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
53	    def compute_discount_cents(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int:
54	        # WHY: BOGO is per-unit, not per-line. A line with quantity 3 is three
55	        #      units. We need at least two units total for one to be free.
56	        unit_prices: list[int] = []
57	
58	        for line in cart.lines:
59	            unit_prices.extend([line.unit_price_cents] * line.quantity)
60	
61	        if len(unit_prices) < 2:
62	            # WHY: with a single unit there is no "get one free" — discount 0,
63	            #      not an error. The cart is valid; it just earns nothing here.
64	            return 0
65	
66	        # WHY: cheapest unit is free. Min over units, capped at subtotal for
67	        #      safety (it always is, but the clamp keeps the contract uniform).
68	        return min(min(unit_prices), cart.subtotal_cents)
69	
70	
71	# ── WHY THIS FACTORY ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	# Translate the enum on the coupon into the matching strategy object. This is a
73	# real Factory: its job is type-to-type translation (DiscountType -> Strategy),
74	# not just `new Foo()`. The service calls this once and keeps the map.
75	# 🎙️ "A tiny factory turns the coupon's kind into a strategy. The mapping lives
76	#     in one place, so a new kind is one new row here and one new class above."
77	def default_strategy_registry() -> dict[DiscountType, DiscountStrategy]:
78	    return {
79	        DiscountType.PERCENT_OFF: PercentOffStrategy(),
80	        DiscountType.FLAT_OFF: FlatOffStrategy(),
81	        DiscountType.BOGO: BogoStrategy(),
82	    }
83

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""The coupon orchestrator: a clean single-threaded core plus a thread-safe
3	subclass that overrides ONE hook.
4	
5	Two patterns are visible here:
6	
7	1. Chain of Responsibility — validation is a chain of handlers
8	   (Expiry -> MinCart -> PerUserLimit -> GlobalLimit). Each handler checks one
9	   rule and passes the request down the chain. Adding a rule is a new handler;
10	   the order is data, not code.
11	
12	2. Template Method (the no-op lock hook) — the core calls `_lock(coupon)` which
13	   does nothing. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY that hook with a real
14	   per-coupon RLock. The core file has zero lock code.
15	
16	The race this design targets: when the LAST use of a usage-limited coupon is
17	claimed by many threads at once, exactly `limit` redemptions must succeed and
18	the rest must get a typed `LimitExhausted`. The atomic check-and-decrement
19	lives in `Coupon.try_consume`; the thread-safe subclass makes the call site
20	atomic with a per-coupon lock.
21	"""
22	
23	import itertools
24	import threading
25	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
26	from collections.abc import Iterator
27	from contextlib import contextmanager
28	from datetime import datetime
29	
30	from models import (
31	    Cart,
32	    CartTooSmall,
33	    Coupon,
34	    CouponConfigError,
35	    CouponExpired,
36	    DiscountType,
37	    LimitExhausted,
38	    PerUserLimitReached,
39	    Redemption,
40	    UnknownCoupon,
41	)
42	from strategies import DiscountStrategy, default_strategy_registry
43	
44	
45	# ── WHY THIS INTERFACE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
46	# A validation handler checks exactly one rule, then hands off to the next.
47	# The chain is built once; each link is small and testable in isolation.
48	# 🎙️ "Each rule is its own handler. The chain runs Expiry, then MinCart, then
49	#     per-user, then global. A new rule is a new link — I don't touch the rest."
50	class ValidationHandler(ABC):
51	    def __init__(self) -> None:
52	        self._next: ValidationHandler | None = None
53	
54	    def set_next(self, handler: "ValidationHandler") -> "ValidationHandler":
55	        # WHY: return the handler we were given so callers can chain the wiring
56	        #      fluently: a.set_next(b).set_next(c). Reads like the rule order.
57	        self._next = handler
58	        return handler
59	
60	    def validate(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
61	        # WHY: run THIS link's check, then pass down. A handler that finds a
62	        #      violation raises a typed error and the chain stops there.
63	        self._check(coupon, cart, now)
64	
65	        if self._next is not None:
66	            self._next.validate(coupon, cart, now)
67	
68	    @abstractmethod
69	    def _check(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
70	        """Raise a typed CouponError if this one rule is violated."""
71	        raise NotImplementedError
72	
73	
74	class ExpiryHandler(ValidationHandler):
75	    def _check(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
76	        # WHY: a coupon used exactly at its expiry instant is still valid; only
77	        #      strictly-past instants are rejected. now is injected so a test
78	        #      can fast-forward without sleeping.
79	        if now > coupon.expires_at:
80	            raise CouponExpired(f"coupon {coupon.code} expired at {coupon.expires_at}")
81	
82	
83	class MinCartHandler(ValidationHandler):
84	    def _check(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
85	        if cart.subtotal_cents < coupon.min_cart_cents:
86	            raise CartTooSmall(
87	                f"cart {cart.subtotal_cents} below minimum {coupon.min_cart_cents}"
88	            )
89	
90	
91	class PerUserLimitHandler(ValidationHandler):
92	    def _check(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
93	        # WHY: read-only pre-check so an over-limit user gets a clean reject
94	        #      BEFORE we try to consume. The authoritative enforcement still
95	        #      happens inside Coupon.try_consume under the lock.
96	        if coupon.user_uses(cart.user_id) >= coupon.per_user_limit:
97	            raise PerUserLimitReached(
98	                f"user {cart.user_id} reached per-user limit for {coupon.code}"
99	            )
100	
101	
102	class GlobalLimitHandler(ValidationHandler):
103	    def _check(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart, now: datetime) -> None:
104	        # WHY: a read-only "looks exhausted" pre-check. It is NOT the atomic
105	        #      guard — two threads can both pass this and then both reach
106	        #      try_consume. The real all-or-nothing decrement is in the coupon,
107	        #      guarded by the lock. This link just rejects the obvious cases
108	        #      early and keeps the error message uniform.
109	        if coupon.remaining_uses <= 0:
110	            raise LimitExhausted(f"coupon {coupon.code} has no remaining uses")
111	
112	
113	def build_default_chain() -> ValidationHandler:
114	    # WHY: order matters and reads as policy — cheap/static checks first
115	    #      (expiry, cart size), then the stateful caps. The head is returned.
116	    head = ExpiryHandler()
117	    head.set_next(MinCartHandler()).set_next(PerUserLimitHandler()).set_next(
118	        GlobalLimitHandler()
119	    )
120	    return head
121	
122	
123	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
124	# The orchestrator — the public API. It owns the coupon catalog, the strategy
125	# map, and the validation chain, and it is the single place a redemption flows
126	# through. The core is single-threaded and lock-free on purpose; concurrency is
127	# a deliberate second version below.
128	# 🎙️ "This is the brain. It holds the coupons, the strategies, and the rule
129	#     chain. Redeem is the one interesting method — validate, price, consume."
130	class CouponService:
131	    def __init__(
132	        self,
133	        strategies: dict[DiscountType, DiscountStrategy] | None = None,
134	        chain: ValidationHandler | None = None,
135	        now_fn=datetime.now,
136	    ) -> None:
137	        # WHY: inject strategies, the chain, and the clock. Defaults keep the
138	        #      happy path one line; tests swap any of them. now_fn lets expiry
139	        #      tests jump forward without real waiting.
140	        self._coupons: dict[str, Coupon] = {}
141	        self._strategies = strategies or default_strategy_registry()
142	        self._chain = chain or build_default_chain()
143	        self._now = now_fn
144	        self._ids = itertools.count(1)
145	
146	    # ── public API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
147	
148	    def create_coupon(self, coupon: Coupon) -> str:
149	        # WHY: reject a duplicate code up front. Two coupons sharing a code
150	        #      would make redeem ambiguous — fail loud at creation instead.
151	        if coupon.code in self._coupons:
152	            raise CouponConfigError(f"coupon code {coupon.code} already exists")
153	
154	        if coupon.discount_type not in self._strategies:
155	            raise CouponConfigError(
156	                f"no strategy registered for {coupon.discount_type}"
157	            )
158	
159	        self._coupons[coupon.code] = coupon
160	        return coupon.code
161	
162	    def get_coupon(self, code: str) -> Coupon:
163	        coupon = self._coupons.get(code)
164	
165	        if coupon is None:
166	            raise UnknownCoupon(f"no coupon with code {code}")
167	
168	        return coupon
169	
170	    def quote(self, code: str, cart: Cart) -> int:
171	        """Validate and price a coupon WITHOUT consuming a use (a preview)."""
172	        coupon = self.get_coupon(code)
173	        now = self._now()
174	        # WHY: run the full rule chain so a preview rejects for the same reasons
175	        #      a redeem would — the customer sees the real outcome before paying.
176	        self._chain.validate(coupon, cart, now)
177	        return self._discount_for(coupon, cart)
178	
179	    def redeem(self, code: str, cart: Cart) -> Redemption:
180	        """Validate, price, and consume one use of the coupon, atomically.
181	
182	        This is the pipeline and the race-prone method. The validation and
183	        pricing read fields that don't change under contention, so they sit
184	        OUTSIDE the lock. The consume — the check-and-decrement of the global
185	        counter — sits INSIDE the per-coupon lock in the thread-safe subclass.
186	        """
187	        coupon = self.get_coupon(code)
188	        now = self._now()
189	
190	        # WHY: validate first. If any rule fails we raise its typed error and
191	        #      never touch the counter. Safe outside the lock — these reads
192	        #      don't depend on the exact moment the counter changes.
193	        self._chain.validate(coupon, cart, now)
194	
195	        discount = self._discount_for(coupon, cart)
196	
197	        # WHY: the critical section. _lock is a no-op here and a real per-coupon
198	        #      RLock in the thread-safe subclass. try_consume re-checks the
199	        #      global cap INSIDE the lock and decrements — check and act as one
200	        #      atomic step, so exactly `limit` callers win the last uses.
201	        with self._lock(coupon):
202	            coupon.try_consume(cart.user_id)
203	
204	        final = cart.subtotal_cents - discount
205	        return Redemption(
206	            redemption_id=f"rdm-{next(self._ids)}",
207	            coupon_code=coupon.code,
208	            user_id=cart.user_id,
209	            discount_cents=discount,
210	            final_cents=final,
211	        )
212	
213	    # ── private helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
214	
215	    def _discount_for(self, coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int:
216	        # WHY: pick the strategy by the coupon's kind and delegate. The service
217	        #      never branches on discount type — that's the Strategy seam.
218	        strategy = self._strategies[coupon.discount_type]
219	        return strategy.compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart)
220	
221	    # ── concurrency hook (Template Method) ──────────────────────────────────
222	
223	    @contextmanager
224	    def _lock(self, coupon: Coupon) -> Iterator[None]:
225	        # WHY: does nothing in the clean core — zero lock code in this class.
226	        #      The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method. The diff
227	        #      between clean and safe is this one hook.
228	        yield
229	
230	
231	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
232	# The thread-safe version. It changes ONE thing: the _lock hook now takes a real
233	# per-coupon reentrant lock. Everything else — validate, price, consume — is
234	# inherited unchanged. This is the whole concurrency story in ~15 lines.
235	# 🎙️ "The thread-safe service overrides one method. Per-coupon locks, so two
236	#     different coupons redeem in parallel; only the SAME coupon serializes."
237	class ThreadSafeCouponService(CouponService):
238	    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs) -> None:
239	        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
240	        # WHY: one lock per coupon code so different coupons never block each
241	        #      other. _map_lock guards the dict of locks itself — two threads
242	        #      creating the same coupon's lock at once could corrupt the dict.
243	        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
244	        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()
245	
246	    @contextmanager
247	    def _lock(self, coupon: Coupon) -> Iterator[None]:
248	        # WHY: lock the MAP first, get-or-create this coupon's lock, then lock
249	        #      the coupon. RLock because try_consume may sit under a method that
250	        #      already holds it; the same thread re-entering is fine.
251	        with self._map_lock:
252	            lock = self._locks.setdefault(coupon.code, threading.RLock())
253	
254	        with lock:
255	            yield
256

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Narrated demo driver for the coupon system.
3	
4	Run it with:  python main.py
5	Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK" so a green run is obvious.
6	The cases walk the happy path, every typed error, the discount strategies, and
7	the headline concurrency case: N threads racing for the last K uses.
8	"""
9	
10	import threading
11	from datetime import datetime, timedelta
12	
13	from coupon_service import CouponService, ThreadSafeCouponService
14	from models import (
15	    Cart,
16	    CartLine,
17	    CartTooSmall,
18	    Coupon,
19	    CouponExpired,
20	    DiscountType,
21	    LimitExhausted,
22	    PerUserLimitReached,
23	    UnknownCoupon,
24	)
25	
26	
27	def _cart(user_id: str, *lines: tuple[str, int, int]) -> Cart:
28	    return Cart(user_id=user_id, lines=tuple(CartLine(i, p, q) for i, p, q in lines))
29	
30	
31	def main() -> None:
32	    now = datetime(2026, 6, 21, 12, 0, 0)
33	    svc = CouponService(now_fn=lambda: now)
34	
35	    # Case 1 — percent-off happy path.
36	    svc.create_coupon(
37	        Coupon(
38	            code="SAVE20",
39	            discount_type=DiscountType.PERCENT_OFF,
40	            expires_at=now + timedelta(days=7),
41	            min_cart_cents=1000,
42	            per_user_limit=2,
43	            usage_limit=100,
44	            value=20,
45	        )
46	    )
47	    cart = _cart("alice", ("book", 1500, 2))  # subtotal 3000
48	    rdm = svc.redeem("SAVE20", cart)
49	    assert rdm.discount_cents == 600, rdm           # 20% of 3000
50	    assert rdm.final_cents == 2400, rdm
51	    print(f"Case 1 percent-off: 20% of 3000 -> discount {rdm.discount_cents}, "
52	          f"final {rdm.final_cents} -> OK")
53	
54	    # Case 2 — flat-off clamped to subtotal so the bill never goes negative.
55	    svc.create_coupon(
56	        Coupon(
57	            code="FLAT500",
58	            discount_type=DiscountType.FLAT_OFF,
59	            expires_at=now + timedelta(days=7),
60	            min_cart_cents=0,
61	            per_user_limit=5,
62	            usage_limit=100,
63	            value=500,
64	        )
65	    )
66	    small = _cart("bob", ("pen", 300, 1))           # subtotal 300 < 500 off
67	    rdm2 = svc.redeem("FLAT500", small)
68	    assert rdm2.discount_cents == 300 and rdm2.final_cents == 0, rdm2
69	    print(f"Case 2 flat-off clamp: 500-off on 300 cart -> discount "
70	          f"{rdm2.discount_cents}, final {rdm2.final_cents} -> OK")
71	
72	    # Case 3 — BOGO: cheapest of three units is free.
73	    svc.create_coupon(
74	        Coupon(
75	            code="BOGO1",
76	            discount_type=DiscountType.BOGO,
77	            expires_at=now + timedelta(days=7),
78	            min_cart_cents=0,
79	            per_user_limit=5,
80	            usage_limit=100,
81	            value=0,
82	        )
83	    )
84	    mixed = _cart("cara", ("mug", 800, 1), ("cup", 300, 2))  # units: 800,300,300
85	    rdm3 = svc.redeem("BOGO1", mixed)
86	    assert rdm3.discount_cents == 300, rdm3          # cheapest unit free
87	    print(f"Case 3 BOGO: cheapest of (800,300,300) free -> discount "
88	          f"{rdm3.discount_cents} -> OK")
89	
90	    # Case 4 — every typed rejection path.
91	    expired = Coupon(
92	        code="OLD",
93	        discount_type=DiscountType.FLAT_OFF,
94	        expires_at=now - timedelta(days=1),
95	        min_cart_cents=0,
96	        per_user_limit=1,
97	        usage_limit=10,
98	        value=100,
99	    )
100	    svc.create_coupon(expired)
101	    _expect(lambda: svc.redeem("OLD", _cart("d", ("x", 500, 1))), CouponExpired)
102	    _expect(lambda: svc.redeem("NOPE", _cart("d", ("x", 500, 1))), UnknownCoupon)
103	    _expect(lambda: svc.redeem("SAVE20", _cart("e", ("x", 500, 1))), CartTooSmall)
104	
105	    # per-user limit: alice already used SAVE20 once (limit 2). Use it again, ok;
106	    # third time must reject.
107	    svc.redeem("SAVE20", cart)                        # alice's 2nd use -> ok
108	    _expect(lambda: svc.redeem("SAVE20", cart), PerUserLimitReached)
109	    print("Case 4 rejections: expired, unknown, cart-too-small, per-user -> OK")
110	
111	    # Case 5 — the redemption race: N threads, last K uses.
112	    K = 5
113	    N = 40
114	    safe = ThreadSafeCouponService(now_fn=lambda: now)
115	    safe.create_coupon(
116	        Coupon(
117	            code="LASTK",
118	            discount_type=DiscountType.FLAT_OFF,
119	            expires_at=now + timedelta(days=1),
120	            min_cart_cents=0,
121	            per_user_limit=1,
122	            usage_limit=K,
123	            value=100,
124	        )
125	    )
126	    wins: list[str] = []
127	    losses: list[Exception] = []
128	    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
129	    lock = threading.Lock()
130	
131	    def attempt(uid: str) -> None:
132	        c = _cart(uid, ("x", 1000, 1))
133	        barrier.wait()                                # all N start together
134	        try:
135	            r = safe.redeem("LASTK", c)
136	            with lock:
137	                wins.append(r.redemption_id)
138	        except LimitExhausted as exc:
139	            with lock:
140	                losses.append(exc)
141	
142	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"u{i}",)) for i in range(N)]
143	    for t in threads:
144	        t.start()
145	    for t in threads:
146	        t.join()
147	
148	    coupon = safe.get_coupon("LASTK")
149	    assert len(wins) == K, f"expected {K} wins, got {len(wins)}"
150	    assert len(set(wins)) == K, "winning redemption ids must be unique"
151	    assert len(losses) == N - K, f"expected {N - K} losses, got {len(losses)}"
152	    assert coupon.remaining_uses == 0, coupon.remaining_uses
153	    print(f"Case 5 race: {N} threads for last {K} uses -> {len(wins)} win, "
154	          f"{len(losses)} get LimitExhausted, remaining_uses="
155	          f"{coupon.remaining_uses} -> OK")
156	
157	    print("\nAll cases passed.")
158	
159	
160	def _expect(fn, error_type) -> None:
161	    try:
162	        fn()
163	    except error_type:
164	        return
165	    raise AssertionError(f"expected {error_type.__name__} but no error was raised")
166	
167	
168	if __name__ == "__main__":
169	    main()

222	# ── chain wiring ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
223	
224	def test_chain_ordering_expiry_before_cart_size():
225	    # An expired AND too-small cart should fail on EXPIRY first (chain order).
226	    svc = make_service()
227	    svc.create_coupon(
228	        flat("OE", 100, expires_at=NOW - timedelta(days=1), min_cart_cents=99999)
229	    )
230	    with pytest.raises(CouponExpired):
231	        svc.redeem("OE", cart("u", ("x", 1, 1)))
232	
233	
234	def test_build_default_chain_is_usable():
235	    chain = build_default_chain()
236	    coupon = flat("C", 100, min_cart_cents=1000)
237	    with pytest.raises(CartTooSmall):
238	        chain.validate(coupon, cart("u", ("x", 1, 1)), NOW)
239	
240	
241	# ── the concurrency race — STRICT post-state ─────────────────────────────────
242	
243	def test_last_k_uses_race_exactly_k_win():
244	    """N threads redeem the last K uses through a Barrier.
245	
246	    Strict assertions:
247	      - exactly K succeed
248	      - every winning redemption id is UNIQUE
249	      - the N-K losers ALL get the typed LimitExhausted (no surprise errors)
250	      - the coupon's own state confirms exactly K committed (remaining_uses==0,
251	        redeemed_count==K)
252	    A tiny sleep inside the consume path is forced via per-user single use plus
253	    contention; we additionally assert peak concurrency was real by checking no
254	    over-commit happened under the Barrier.
255	    """
256	    K = 5
257	    N = 50
258	    svc = ThreadSafeCouponService(now_fn=lambda: NOW)
259	    svc.create_coupon(flat("RACE", 100, usage_limit=K, per_user_limit=1))
260	
261	    wins: list[Redemption] = []
262	    losses: list[Exception] = []
263	    barrier = threading.Barrier(N)
264	    guard = threading.Lock()
265	
266	    def attempt(uid: str) -> None:
267	        c = cart(uid, ("x", 1000, 1))
268	        barrier.wait()                          # all N hit redeem at one instant
269	        try:
270	            r = svc.redeem("RACE", c)
271	            with guard:
272	                wins.append(r)
273	        except LimitExhausted as exc:
274	            with guard:
275	                losses.append(exc)
276	
277	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"u{i}",)) for i in range(N)]
278	    for t in threads:
279	        t.start()
280	    for t in threads:
281	        t.join()
282	
283	    assert len(wins) == K, f"expected {K} winners, got {len(wins)}"
284	    assert len({w.redemption_id for w in wins}) == K, "winner ids must be unique"
285	    assert len({w.user_id for w in wins}) == K, "winners must be distinct users"
286	    assert len(losses) == N - K, f"expected {N - K} losses, got {len(losses)}"
287	    assert all(isinstance(e, LimitExhausted) for e in losses), "all typed errors"
288	
289	    coupon = svc.get_coupon("RACE")
290	    assert coupon.remaining_uses == 0
291	    assert coupon.redeemed_count == K               # coupon's own state confirms K
292	
293	
294	class _SplitCheckActCoupon(Coupon):
295	    """A coupon whose consume SPLITS the check and the act with a real hold.
296	
297	    This is the crucial bit. With the GIL, a bare `redeemed_count += 1` is hard
298	    to race because the increment is effectively atomic. To prove the lock is
299	    actually load-bearing, we widen the window: read remaining_uses (CHECK),
300	    sleep (which releases the GIL, letting other threads also pass the CHECK),
301	    THEN increment (ACT). Without a lock around this, many threads pass the
302	    check during the sleep and over-commit far past K. With the per-coupon
303	    lock, only one thread is ever inside, so the count stops exactly at K.
304	    """
305	
306	    def try_consume(self, user_id: str) -> None:
307	        remaining = self.remaining_uses        # CHECK
308	        time.sleep(0.003)                       # window: GIL released here
309	        if remaining <= 0:
310	            raise LimitExhausted(f"coupon {self.code} has no remaining uses")
311	        self.redeemed_count += 1                # ACT
312	        self._per_user_counts[user_id] = self.user_uses(user_id) + 1
313	
314	
315	def _run_split_race(service: CouponService, k: int, n: int) -> list[Redemption]:
316	    coupon = _SplitCheckActCoupon(
317	        code="SPLIT",
318	        discount_type=DiscountType.FLAT_OFF,
319	        expires_at=NOW + timedelta(days=1),
320	        min_cart_cents=0,
321	        per_user_limit=1,
322	        usage_limit=k,
323	        value=100,
324	    )
325	    service.create_coupon(coupon)
326	
327	    wins: list[Redemption] = []
328	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
329	    guard = threading.Lock()
330	
331	    def attempt(uid: str) -> None:
332	        c = cart(uid, ("x", 1000, 1))
333	        barrier.wait()
334	        try:
335	            r = service.redeem("SPLIT", c)
336	            with guard:
337	                wins.append(r)
338	        except LimitExhausted:
339	            pass
340	
341	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"u{i}",)) for i in range(n)]
342	    for t in threads:
343	        t.start()
344	    for t in threads:
345	        t.join()
346	
347	    return wins
348	
349	
350	def test_lock_free_core_over_commits_proving_the_race_is_real():
351	    """Negative control: the LOCK-FREE base service MUST over-commit here.
352	
353	    If this assertion ever fails (i.e. the lock-free core also bound at K), the
354	    race window is too narrow and the thread-safe test below would pass
355	    trivially — proving nothing. The split check/act hold guarantees a real
356	    race, so the lock-free core lets MORE than K through.
357	    """
358	    K = 3
359	    N = 30
360	    wins = _run_split_race(CouponService(now_fn=lambda: NOW), K, N)
361	    assert len(wins) > K, (
362	        "lock-free core did not over-commit — the race window is too narrow, "
363	        "so the thread-safe test would be a trivial pass"
364	    )
365	
366	
367	def test_thread_safe_binds_exactly_k_under_widened_race():
368	    """Positive proof: under the SAME widened race, the lock binds at exactly K.
369	
370	    Peak concurrency is real (the negative-control test above shows the
371	    lock-free core over-commits to N under this exact setup); the per-coupon
372	    lock is what holds the line at K.
373	    """
374	    K = 3
375	    N = 30
376	    svc = ThreadSafeCouponService(now_fn=lambda: NOW)
377	    wins = _run_split_race(svc, K, N)
378	
379	    assert len(wins) == K, f"thread-safe over/under-commit: {len(wins)}"
380	    assert len({w.redemption_id for w in wins}) == K
381	    coupon = svc.get_coupon("SPLIT")
382	    assert coupon.remaining_uses == 0
383	    assert coupon.redeemed_count == K

1	# Design a Coupon Management System
2

15	| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — use the hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed CAS, 10M coupons, partial-failure undo). | ❓ IF ASKED |
16	
17	**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
18	- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo cases verbally. Write code but do not execute.
19	- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output. Cases are proved by the program.
20	
21	**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Right after drawing the diagram, say — unprompted — *"I see a redemption race: when the last use of a usage-limited coupon is claimed by many requests at once, a naive check-then-decrement lets more than `limit` through. I'll keep the core clean and show the per-coupon lock fix in §9."* Said early and confidently, this signals you've built real concurrent systems.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
26	
27	### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
28	
29	> 💬 "So this is the coupons engine behind a checkout. Sellers create coupons — 20% off, $5 off, buy-one-get-one — and hang constraints on them: an expiry date, a minimum cart value, a cap on how many times one user can use it, and a global cap on total redemptions. When a customer checks out, my system takes their cart and a coupon code, decides whether the coupon is allowed, computes the discount, and 'spends' one use. The whole thing is in memory and has to be thread-safe — the interesting part is when the very last use of a limited coupon gets grabbed by a bunch of checkouts at the same instant."
30	
31	It's a validation-and-redemption engine, not a catalog UI. The hard part isn't the discount math — it's making "spend exactly `limit` uses, no more" hold under concurrency.
32	
33	### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led)
34	
35	**You:** "I'll treat money as integer cents everywhere — no floats — so discounts are exact and I never get rounding drift. Good for v1?"
36	**Interviewer:** "Yep, cents is fine."
37	*✓ locked: all amounts are `int` cents; strategies do integer math.*
38	
39	**You:** "For discount kinds I'll support percent-off, flat-off, and BOGO where the single cheapest unit is free. Is that the full v1 set, or do you also want stacking multiple coupons?"
40	**Interviewer:** "Those three. No stacking — one coupon per cart."
41	*✓ scoped: one coupon per redemption; stacking is a future Strategy/composite, flag for §9.*
42	
43	**You:** "The constraints I'll enforce, in this order, are: not expired → cart meets the minimum → user hasn't hit their per-user limit → the coupon still has a global use left. If two of those fail, the customer sees the first one in that order. Sound right?"
44	**Interviewer:** "Agreed — order them cheapest-to-check first, which you did."
45	*✓ this is the Chain of Responsibility order: Expiry → MinCart → PerUserLimit → GlobalLimit.*
46	
47	**You:** "When the global limit is hit, I'll reject the over-the-limit redemption with a specific typed error — `LimitExhausted` — not a generic failure, so the caller can show 'this coupon is sold out'. No waitlist or queueing. OK?"
48	**Interviewer:** "Good, clean reject is right."
49	*✓ every redemption ends accept-with-receipt or reject-with-typed-reason; never silently dropped.*
50	
51	**You:** "I'll assume expiry is checked against an injected clock so I can test 'it's now past expiry' without sleeping, and a coupon used at the exact expiry instant still counts as valid — only strictly-past is rejected. Reasonable?"
52	**Interviewer:** "Sure, inject the clock."
53	*✓ `now_fn` injected; boundary = valid AT expiry, invalid AFTER.*
54	
55	**You:** "Out of scope for this round: who delivers the coupon to the user, the seller-management UI, persistence/DB, and analytics. I'm building the in-memory validate-and-redeem core. Agreed?"
56	**Interviewer:** "Agreed."
57	*✓ scope cut — surfaces in §1.3 Out of scope.*
58	
59	### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
60	
61	**Functional Requirements**
62	
63	1. **A seller creates a coupon** with a kind (percent / flat / BOGO), a value, and constraints (expiry, min cart cents, per-user limit, global usage limit).
64	   - Returns the coupon code on success; rejects a duplicate code or contradictory config (e.g. percent > 100, usage_limit ≤ 0) with a typed error.
65	2. **A customer previews a coupon against a cart** (`quote`) — runs all rules and returns the discount in cents **without** spending a use.
66	   - Same rejection reasons as redeem; read-only.
67	3. **A customer redeems a coupon against a cart** (`redeem`) — validates all rules, computes the discount, spends exactly one global use, and returns a receipt.
68	   - Returns a `Redemption` (discount + final amount) on success.
69	   - Rejects with the specific typed error for the first failing rule: expired, cart too small, per-user limit reached, or global limit exhausted; unknown code is its own error.
70	4. **The system enforces the global usage limit atomically** — under concurrent redemptions of the last few uses, exactly `usage_limit` succeed in total and the rest get `LimitExhausted`; `remaining_uses` never goes negative.
71	
72	**Out of scope**
73	- Coupon delivery / who shows the code to the user (downstream — we just validate and spend).
74	- Seller-management UI and persistence/DB (we're the in-memory core).
75	- Coupon stacking / combining multiple coupons on one cart (separate composite feature — flag in §9).
76	- Analytics, fraud scoring, A/B targeting (separate systems).
77	
78	> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything I want to lock down the rule order — Expiry, then minimum cart, then per-user, then the global limit — and that the global limit is the one with the concurrency teeth. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."
79	
80	---
81	
82	## 2. Actors & Core Flows
83	
84	**Actors**
85	- **Seller** — creates coupons with constraints. Calls `create_coupon`.
86	- **Customer (checkout)** — previews and redeems a coupon against their cart. Calls `quote` and `redeem`.
87	- **CouponService** — the orchestrator that owns the catalog, runs the rule chain, prices via a strategy, and spends a use.
88	
89	**Core flows**
90	1. Seller: build a `Coupon` → `create_coupon` → code registered in the catalog.
91	2. Customer preview: `quote(code, cart)` → run rule chain → strategy prices it → return discount cents, no use spent.
92	3. Customer redeem: `redeem(code, cart)` → run rule chain → price → atomically spend one global use → return `Redemption` receipt.
93	4. Redemption rejection: any failing rule → first typed error (`CouponExpired` / `CartTooSmall` / `PerUserLimitReached` / `LimitExhausted`) → no use spent.
94	
95	---
96	
97	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
98	
99	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
100	
101	> **Coupon** — A real thing with state (remaining uses, per-user tallies) AND behavior (decide if a use can be spent, spend it). It's referenced by every redemption and owns the "never below zero" rule. *Accepted — it's the state machine at the heart of the design.*
102	>
103	> **Cart** — The thing we validate and price against. It carries the user id and the line items, and the subtotal is derived from them. It has a clear identity for one checkout. *Accepted as a frozen value object — we evaluate a snapshot, we don't mutate the live cart.*
104	>
105	> **CartLine** — One item + unit price + quantity. It's pure data with a tiny derived `line_total`. Could be a tuple, but giving it a class lets it validate itself (no negative price, quantity ≥ 1). *Accepted as a frozen value object.*
106	>
107	> **Redemption** — The result of a successful redeem: which coupon, how much came off, the final total. It's a receipt — issued once, never changed. *Accepted as a frozen value object.*
108	>
109	> **DiscountStrategy** — Not a noun from the prompt, but the percent/flat/BOGO behavior varies and the service must not branch on type. *Accepted as a Strategy interface with three concretes.*
110	>
111	> **ValidationHandler** — Each constraint (expiry, min cart, per-user, global) is one rule that can pass or reject. Modeling each as a handler in a chain keeps the order as data and each rule independently testable. *Accepted as a Chain-of-Responsibility interface with four concretes.*
112	>
113	> **Seller / Customer** — Actors. They CALL the system; the system doesn't store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.* (The customer's id rides on the `Cart`.)
114	>
115	> **DiscountType** — A fixed set of values (PERCENT_OFF / FLAT_OFF / BOGO) with no behavior of its own. *Rejected as an entity — modeled as an enum that selects a strategy.*
116	>
117	> **Expiry / MinCartValue / PerUserLimit / UsageLimit** — These are constraints, i.e. fields on `Coupon`, enforced by handlers. *Rejected as entities — they're typed fields plus one handler each.*
118	>
119	> **CouponCode** — Tempting to wrap, but it's just the string key into the catalog map with no lifecycle of its own. *Rejected — a `str` field and the dict key.*
120	
121	### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
122	
123	| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
124	|---|---|---|---|
125	| `Coupon` | Owns its usage counters and the atomic spend | `code: str` (id), `discount_type: DiscountType`, `expires_at: datetime`, `min_cart_cents: int`, `per_user_limit: int`, `usage_limit: int`, `value: int`, `redeemed_count: int`, `_per_user_counts: dict[str,int]` | `0 <= redeemed_count <= usage_limit` always; a spend is all-or-nothing |
126	| `Cart` | A frozen snapshot to validate/price | `user_id: str`, `lines: tuple[CartLine,...]`; `subtotal_cents` derived | non-empty; subtotal is derived, never stored stale |
127	| `CartLine` | One priced line | `item_id: str`, `unit_price_cents: int`, `quantity: int`; `line_total_cents` derived | `unit_price_cents >= 0`, `quantity >= 1` |
128	| `Redemption` | The immutable receipt | `redemption_id: str` (id), `coupon_code: str`, `user_id: str`, `discount_cents: int`, `final_cents: int` | frozen — a receipt never changes |
129	
130	**Enums**
131	- `DiscountType`: `PERCENT_OFF`, `FLAT_OFF`, `BOGO`.
132	
133	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)
134	
135	> 💬 "So the model has four objects plus the service. `CouponService` is the brain — it owns the catalog of coupons, the strategy map, and the validation chain, and every redemption flows through it. `Coupon` is the interesting one: it owns its remaining-uses counter and the one method that spends a use, so the 'never go below zero' rule lives in exactly one place — the service never touches the counter by hand. `Cart` and `CartLine` are frozen value objects — we price a snapshot, we don't mutate the customer's live cart — and `Redemption` is the receipt we hand back, also frozen. The things I deliberately left OUT are the seller and the customer — they're actors that call the API, not stored entities — and the constraints like expiry and the limits, which are just typed fields on the coupon plus one validation handler each."
136	
137	---
138	
139	## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)
140	
141	| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
142	|---|---|---|---|---|
143	| `create_coupon` | `(coupon: Coupon) -> str` | the registered coupon code | `CouponConfigError` | A seller registers a coupon; rejects a duplicate code or unknown discount kind |
144	| `get_coupon` | `(code: str) -> Coupon` | the coupon | `UnknownCoupon` | Look up a coupon by code (used by quote/redeem and by callers to inspect state) |
145	| `quote` | `(code: str, cart: Cart) -> int` | discount in cents | `UnknownCoupon`, `CouponExpired`, `CartTooSmall`, `PerUserLimitReached`, `LimitExhausted` | Customer previews the discount; runs all rules but spends NO use |
146	| `redeem` | `(code: str, cart: Cart) -> Redemption` | the receipt (discount + final) | `UnknownCoupon`, `CouponExpired`, `CartTooSmall`, `PerUserLimitReached`, `LimitExhausted` | Customer redeems; validates, prices, and spends exactly one global use atomically |
147	
148	> 🎙️ "The whole system is four public calls — one to register a coupon, one read, and the two that matter: `quote` previews without spending, `redeem` validates-prices-and-spends as one operation. Everything else — which rule runs, which discount strategy fires, how the use is spent atomically — is internal. If you handed me this table cold, you'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a class. And the `Raises` column already tells you the four ways a redemption can be rejected."
149	
150	---
151	
152	## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
153	
154	### 4.1 Per-class derivation
155	
156	#### CouponService (the orchestrator)
157	
158	**(a) One-line role** — the public API: external code calls it to create, quote, and redeem coupons; every redemption flows through here.
159	
160	**(b) State derivation**
161	
162	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
163	|---|---|
164	| "A seller creates a coupon … returns the code" | A catalog: `code -> Coupon` |
165	| "compute the discount" (3 kinds) | A map: `DiscountType -> DiscountStrategy` |
166	| "validates … in this order" | The head of the validation chain |
167	| "checked against an injected clock" | A `now_fn` clock |
168	| numbered receipt ids | An id counter |
169	
170	```
171	class CouponService:
172	    - _coupons: dict[str, Coupon]
173	    - _strategies: dict[DiscountType, DiscountStrategy]
174	    - _chain: ValidationHandler
175	    - _now: () -> datetime
176	    - _ids: counter
177	```
178	
179	**State placement trade-off** — the per-user and global use counts could live on the service (relational) or on the `Coupon` (intrinsic). I put them on the `Coupon`: a coupon's usage limit is *intrinsic* to that coupon's policy, not a relationship the service manages. That keeps the atomic spend on the one object that owns the counter, so the lock has a natural per-coupon granularity.
180	
181	**(c) Operations derivation**
182	
183	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
184	|---|---|
185	| "A seller creates a coupon" | `create_coupon(coupon) -> code \| error` |
186	| look up / inspect a coupon | `get_coupon(code) -> Coupon \| error` |
187	| "preview … without spending a use" | `quote(code, cart) -> int \| error` |
188	| "validates, prices, spends one use" | `redeem(code, cart) -> Redemption \| error` |
189	
190	```
191	class CouponService:
192	    - _coupons: dict[str, Coupon]
193	    - _strategies: dict[DiscountType, DiscountStrategy]
194	    - _chain: ValidationHandler
195	    - _now: () -> datetime
196	    - _ids: counter
197	
198	    + CouponService(strategies=None, chain=None, now_fn=datetime.now)
199	    + create_coupon(coupon: Coupon) -> str
200	    + get_coupon(code: str) -> Coupon
201	    + quote(code: str, cart: Cart) -> int
202	    + redeem(code: str, cart: Cart) -> Redemption
203	    - _discount_for(coupon, cart) -> int
204	    # _lock(coupon) -> contextmanager   # no-op hook; thread-safe subclass overrides
205	```
206	
207	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
208	- **Why is the validation chain injected, not hardcoded?** The rule order is policy. Injecting the chain head means a test (or a new market) can reorder or add rules without touching `redeem`.
209	- **Why does `redeem` price the discount OUTSIDE the lock but spend the use INSIDE?** Pricing reads fields that don't change under contention (the coupon's value, the cart). Only the global-counter check-and-decrement is racy, so only that sits in the critical section — the lock is held for microseconds.
210	- **Why `quote` and `redeem` share the chain?** So a preview rejects for exactly the same reasons a redeem would — the customer never sees "looks fine" then a surprise reject at spend time.
211	
212	#### Coupon (the counter state machine)
213	
214	**(a) One-line role** — owns the usage counters and the single atomic method that spends a use; the only object allowed to change the count.
215	
216	**(b) State derivation**
217	
218	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
219	|---|---|
220	| kind + value | `discount_type`, `value` |
221	| "expiry, min cart value" | `expires_at`, `min_cart_cents` |
222	| "per-user limit, global usage limit" | `per_user_limit`, `usage_limit` |
223	| "how many uses are left" | `redeemed_count` (remaining is derived) |
224	| "this user has used it N times" | `_per_user_counts: dict[str,int]` |
225	
226	```
227	class Coupon:
228	    - code: str
229	    - discount_type: DiscountType
230	    - expires_at: datetime
231	    - min_cart_cents: int
232	    - per_user_limit: int
233	    - usage_limit: int
234	    - value: int
235	    - redeemed_count: int = 0
236	    - _per_user_counts: dict[str, int]
237	```
238	
239	**(c) Operations derivation**
240	
241	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
242	|---|---|
243	| "how many uses are left" | `remaining_uses -> int` (property) |
244	| "how many times has this user used it" | `user_uses(user_id) -> int` |
245	| "spend one use, atomically, or fail" | `try_consume(user_id) -> None \| error` |
246	
247	```
248	class Coupon:
249	    - code: str
250	    - discount_type: DiscountType
251	    - expires_at: datetime
252	    - min_cart_cents: int
253	    - per_user_limit: int
254	    - usage_limit: int
255	    - value: int
256	    - redeemed_count: int = 0
257	    - _per_user_counts: dict[str, int]
258	
259	    + Coupon(code, discount_type, expires_at, min_cart_cents,
260	             per_user_limit, usage_limit, value)
261	    + remaining_uses -> int                # property: usage_limit - redeemed_count
262	    + user_uses(user_id: str) -> int
263	    + try_consume(user_id: str) -> None     # check global + per-user, then bump both
264	```
265	
266	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
267	- **Why is `remaining_uses` derived, not stored?** One number (`redeemed_count`) plus the limit gives both views. A stored `remaining` could drift out of sync with the count; deriving it makes that impossible.
268	- **Why does `try_consume` check the global cap FIRST, then per-user?** The global cap is the shared, racy resource — we reject the exhausted case before touching the per-user tally. Both move together only on success, so the state is never half-updated.
269	- **Why does the Coupon own the spend instead of the service?** Information Expert: the coupon has the count, so it enforces the count. The thread-safe service just wraps the *call site* in a per-coupon lock; the rule itself never moves.
270	
271	#### Cart (frozen value object)
272	
273	**(a) One-line role** — the snapshot we validate and price against; carries the user and the lines.
274	
275	**(b) State derivation**
276	
277	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
278	|---|---|
279	| "per-user limit" needs to know who | `user_id` |
280	| "min cart value", pricing | `lines` (subtotal derived) |
281	
282	```
283	class Cart:
284	    - user_id: str
285	    - lines: tuple[CartLine, ...]
286	```

288	**(c) Operations derivation**
289	
290	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
291	|---|---|
292	| "cart meets the minimum", percent/flat pricing | `subtotal_cents -> int` (property) |
293	
294	```
295	class Cart:                          # @dataclass(frozen=True)
296	    - user_id: str
297	    - lines: tuple[CartLine, ...]
298	
299	    + Cart(user_id, lines)           # __post_init__ rejects empty cart
300	    + subtotal_cents -> int          # property: sum of line totals
301	```

303	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
304	- **Why frozen?** We're pricing a snapshot for one redemption. Freezing means the subtotal we validated is the subtotal we charge — no mid-flight mutation.
305	- **Why a tuple of lines, not a list?** A tuple makes the frozen guarantee real (a list field on a frozen dataclass is still mutable in place).

307	#### CartLine (frozen value object)
308	
309	**(a) One-line role** — one priced line: item, unit price, quantity.
310	
311	**(b) State derivation**
312	
313	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
314	|---|---|
315	| pricing per item | `item_id`, `unit_price_cents`, `quantity` |

318	class CartLine:
319	    - item_id: str
320	    - unit_price_cents: int
321	    - quantity: int

324	**(c) Operations derivation**
325	
326	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
327	|---|---|
328	| subtotal, BOGO unit expansion | `line_total_cents -> int` (property) |

330	```
331	class CartLine:                      # @dataclass(frozen=True)
332	    - item_id: str
333	    - unit_price_cents: int
334	    - quantity: int
335	
336	    + CartLine(item_id, unit_price_cents, quantity)   # rejects neg price / qty<1
337	    + line_total_cents -> int        # property: unit_price_cents * quantity

340	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
341	- **Why validate in `__post_init__`?** A negative price or zero quantity is a contradictory input — reject it the moment the value is built, with a typed error, not three layers deep.

345	**(a) One-line role** — the immutable receipt returned by a successful `redeem`.

351	| "returns a receipt (discount + final)" | the coupon, user, discount, final |

354	class Redemption:

356	    - coupon_code: str
357	    - user_id: str
358	    - discount_cents: int
359	    - final_cents: int

363	
364	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
365	|---|---|
366	| (none — it's a receipt) | — |

369	class Redemption:                    # @dataclass(frozen=True)
370	    - redemption_id: str
371	    - coupon_code: str
372	    - user_id: str
373	    - discount_cents: int

376	    + Redemption(redemption_id, coupon_code, user_id, discount_cents, final_cents)

380	- **Why a class and not just a tuple return?** Named fields read better at the call site and the frozen guarantee says "this is a fact, don't mutate it."

382	#### DiscountStrategy (Strategy interface) + concretes
383	
384	**(a) One-line role** — the seam that lets each discount kind compute itself; the service never branches on type.
385	
386	**(c) Operations**
387	
388	| Need from requirements | Method on the interface |
389	|---|---|
390	| "compute the discount" for each kind | `compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart) -> int` |
391	
392	```
393	interface DiscountStrategy:                 # ABC
394	    + compute_discount_cents(coupon: Coupon, cart: Cart) -> int
395	
396	class PercentOffStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
397	    + compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart) -> int   # subtotal*value//100, clamped
398	
399	class FlatOffStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
400	    + compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart) -> int   # min(value, subtotal)
401	
402	class BogoStrategy(DiscountStrategy):
403	    + compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart) -> int   # cheapest unit free, clamped
404	```
405	
406	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
407	- **Why integer math (`//`)?** Money in cents; floor division rounds in the merchant's favor and avoids float drift.
408	- **Why is BOGO per-unit, not per-line?** A line of quantity 3 is three units; we need ≥ 2 units total for one to be free, so we expand lines into units first.
409	
410	#### ValidationHandler (Chain of Responsibility interface) + concretes
411	
412	**(a) One-line role** — each rule is one handler; the chain runs them in order and the first violation raises its typed error.
413	
414	**(c) Operations**
415	
416	| Need from requirements | Method on the interface |
417	|---|---|
418	| "check this one rule, then pass down" | `validate(coupon, cart, now) -> None` |
419	| wiring the order | `set_next(handler) -> handler` |
420	
421	```
422	interface ValidationHandler:                # ABC
423	    - _next: ValidationHandler | None
424	    + set_next(handler) -> ValidationHandler
425	    + validate(coupon, cart, now) -> None    # _check then delegate to _next
426	    # _check(coupon, cart, now) -> None       (abstract; each handler's one rule)
427	
428	class ExpiryHandler(ValidationHandler):       # raises CouponExpired
429	class MinCartHandler(ValidationHandler):      # raises CartTooSmall
430	class PerUserLimitHandler(ValidationHandler): # raises PerUserLimitReached
431	class GlobalLimitHandler(ValidationHandler):  # raises LimitExhausted (pre-check)
432	```
433	
434	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
435	- **Why a chain instead of four `if`s in `redeem`?** The order is policy and each rule is independently testable. Adding "must be a first-time customer" is a new handler, not an edit to `redeem`.
436	- **Why is `GlobalLimitHandler` only a pre-check, not the enforcement?** Two threads can both pass this read-only check. The *authoritative* atomic decrement is `Coupon.try_consume` under the lock — the handler just rejects the obvious-exhausted case early with a uniform message.

526	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
527	
528	### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)
529	
530	> 💬 "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `CouponService` owns the catalog, the strategy map, and the chain because it's the only thing that sees all of them. `Coupon` owns its usage counters and the spend, because the 'never below zero' rule is intrinsic to the coupon — the service never edits the count by hand. `Cart` owns its subtotal because the lines are its data. That single decision — counters live on the `Coupon` — is what gives the concurrency fix a natural per-coupon lock granularity."
531	
532	### 5.2 Applied patterns
533	
534	**Strategy — `DiscountStrategy`**
535	- **Why it fits HERE:** the discount math is exactly the thing that varies and that the business will extend — percent today, BOGO today, "spend $X get $Y" tomorrow. Branching on type inside `redeem` would mean editing the hot path for every new kind.
536	- **Interface + impl:** `DiscountStrategy.compute_discount_cents(coupon, cart) -> int` → `PercentOffStrategy`, `FlatOffStrategy`, `BogoStrategy`.
537	- **🎙️ Script:** "The service holds a map from kind to strategy and just asks the strategy to price the cart. New kind, new class, one map entry — `redeem` never changes."
538	- **💬 Layman gloss:** "The cashier doesn't know the deal math — it asks the coupon's rule-of-the-day to do the sum, so we can add new deals without rewiring the register."
539	
540	**Chain of Responsibility — `ValidationHandler`**
541	- **Why it fits HERE:** the constraints are an *ordered* set of independent checks, and the order is policy ("show expiry before cart-too-small"). A chain makes the order data, each rule a small testable unit, and adding a rule additive.
542	- **Interface + impl:** `ValidationHandler.validate(coupon, cart, now)` → `ExpiryHandler` → `MinCartHandler` → `PerUserLimitHandler` → `GlobalLimitHandler`.
543	- **🎙️ Script:** "Each rule is a link that checks one thing and passes down; the first violation raises its typed error and the chain stops."
544	- **💬 Layman gloss:** "It's a line of inspectors — each checks one thing and waves you on; the first one who says no sends you back, and you hear exactly why."
545	
546	**Template Method — the `_lock` hook**
547	- **Why it fits HERE:** I want the clean core to have zero lock code AND a thread-safe version whose diff is one method. A no-op hook the subclass overrides does exactly that.
548	- **Interface + impl:** `CouponService._lock(coupon)` yields (no-op) → `ThreadSafeCouponService._lock(coupon)` takes a per-coupon `RLock`.
549	- **🎙️ Script:** "The core wraps the spend in `with self._lock(coupon)`, which does nothing. The thread-safe subclass overrides only `_lock`."
550	- **💬 Layman gloss:** "The core leaves a labeled slot that says 'put a lock here if you need one.' The safe version drops a lock into that slot and changes nothing else."
551	
552	### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific)
553	- **Single Responsibility:** `Coupon` owns usage counters, `DiscountStrategy` owns pricing, `ValidationHandler` owns one rule each, `CouponService` owns coordination. Four reasons to change, four places.
554	- **Open/Closed:** a new discount kind is a new `DiscountStrategy`; a new constraint is a new `ValidationHandler`. Zero edits to `redeem`.
555	- **Dependency Inversion:** `CouponService` depends on the `DiscountStrategy` and `ValidationHandler` abstractions, never on `PercentOffStrategy` or `ExpiryHandler` directly.
556	
557	### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
558	Every claim above is wired in §6: Strategy is a real ABC with three impls and a registry the service consults; Chain is a real linked list of handlers with `set_next`; Template Method is a real `@contextmanager` no-op overridden in a subclass. **Stacking multiple coupons** is an extension point (not wired in v1) — I'd add a `CompositeStrategy` or a stacking policy then. I do *not* claim Singleton (the service is just instantiated normally — that's a Facade-ish entry point, not an enforced single instance) or Factory for plain construction (the one real Factory is `default_strategy_registry`, which translates enum → strategy).
559

561	
562	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
563	
564	### A. The Key Insight
565	
566	> **Insight:** Make the `Coupon` own its remaining-uses counter and expose ONE atomic `try_consume` — the global-limit check and the decrement as a single step — so "spend exactly `limit` uses" becomes a property of the object, not of the caller.
567	>
568	> **Why it works:** The race is entirely about one shared integer (`redeemed_count`) and one rule (`< usage_limit`). If the check and the decrement live in one method on the one object that owns the integer, then guarding *that object* with a lock makes the whole check-then-act atomic. Validation and pricing read fields that don't change under contention, so they stay outside the lock — the critical section is two lines.
569	>
570	> **What breaks without it:** The naive version spreads it out — `redeem` reads `coupon.remaining_uses`, decides it's fine, then writes `coupon.redeemed_count += 1` two lines later. Under load, ten checkouts all read `remaining = 1`, all decide "OK", all increment — now `redeemed_count` is 11 on a `usage_limit` of 1, eleven customers got a "sold out" coupon, and finance reconciliation finds the over-spend a week later. By moving the check-and-decrement into `Coupon.try_consume` and locking per coupon, the over-spend is impossible by construction.
571	
572	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
573	
574	**`redeem(code, cart)` — the pipeline (and the race-prone method)**
575	
576	**(1) Pseudocode**
577	```
578	1. coupon = catalog[code]              ; raise UnknownCoupon if missing
579	2. now = clock()
580	3. run validation chain(coupon, cart, now):
581	      a. if now > expires_at -> raise CouponExpired
582	      b. if subtotal < min_cart -> raise CartTooSmall
583	      c. if user_uses(user) >= per_user_limit -> raise PerUserLimitReached
584	      d. if remaining_uses <= 0 -> raise LimitExhausted   (pre-check, read-only)
585	4. discount = strategy_for(coupon.kind).compute(coupon, cart)   ; outside lock
586	5. --- CRITICAL SECTION (per-coupon lock) ---
587	6.    coupon.try_consume(user):
588	7.       if remaining_uses <= 0 -> raise LimitExhausted   (re-check INSIDE lock)
589	8.       if user_uses(user) >= per_user_limit -> raise PerUserLimitReached
590	9.       redeemed_count += 1 ; per_user_counts[user] += 1
591	10. --- END CRITICAL SECTION ---
592	11. final = subtotal - discount
593	12. return Redemption(new id, code, user, discount, final)
594	```
595	
596	**(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script (race-prone method):** "This method has the check-then-act race, and steps 6–9 are the critical section. Steps 1–4 are safe outside any lock — the lookup, the chain, and the pricing read fields that don't change while we spend. The non-obvious step is **step 7**: even though step 3d already checked the global limit, I check it *again* inside the lock. That's deliberate — the step-3 check is a friendly early reject, but two threads can both pass it; the step-7 re-check under the lock is the one that's authoritative. Step 9 is where both counters move together — all-or-nothing. And notice the discount in step 4 is computed *before* the lock, so a slow strategy never holds the critical section."
597	
598	**(3) DRY RUN — `redeem("LASTK", cart(user="u7", [("x",1000,1)]))` when `usage_limit=5, redeemed_count=4`:**
599	
600	| Step | Action | State change |
601	|---|---|---|
602	| 1 | look up LASTK | found ✓ |
603	| 3a–c | expiry / min-cart / per-user | all pass (u7 unused, no minimum) |
604	| 3d | remaining_uses pre-check | `5 - 4 = 1 > 0` ✓ |
605	| 4 | FlatOffStrategy.compute | discount = min(100, 1000) = 100 |
606	| 5 | acquire per-coupon lock | only this thread inside |
607	| 7 | re-check remaining_uses | `1 > 0` ✓ |
608	| 9 | bump counters | `redeemed_count: 4 → 5`, `_per_user_counts["u7"]: 0 → 1` |
609	| 10 | release lock | `remaining_uses` now 0 |
610	| 12 | return receipt | `Redemption(rdm-N, "LASTK", "u7", 100, 900)` |
611	
612	> *Edge probe: a second checkout calls `redeem("LASTK", ...)` while this one is between step 4 and step 9.* *Without the lock both read `remaining=1` at step 3d/7 and both increment → `redeemed_count=6`, over-spend. With the per-coupon lock, the second thread blocks at step 5, and when it runs step 7 it sees `remaining_uses=0` and gets `LimitExhausted`. That's §9.*
613	
614	**`quote(code, cart)` — the preview (lookup + price, no spend)**
615	```
616	1. coupon = catalog[code]              ; raise UnknownCoupon if missing
617	2. run validation chain (same as redeem steps 3a-d)
618	3. return strategy_for(coupon.kind).compute(coupon, cart)
619	```
620	**🎙️ (lookup-shaped):** "This is the read-only twin of `redeem` — same chain, same pricing, but it stops before the spend. Customers see the real discount and the real rejection reasons without burning a use."
621	
622	**`create_coupon(coupon)` — registration**
623	```
624	1. if coupon.code in catalog -> raise CouponConfigError (duplicate)
625	2. if coupon.kind not in strategy map -> raise CouponConfigError
626	3. catalog[code] = coupon ; return code
627	```
628	
629	**`Coupon.try_consume(user)` — the atomic spend** (steps 6–9 above; it IS the critical-section body).
630	
631	### C. Data Structure Choices
632	
633	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative) | Complexity |
634	|---|---|---|---|
635	| `dict[str, Coupon]` | coupon catalog | redeem/quote/get are keyed by code — O(1) hit; a list would be O(n) per lookup on the hot path | O(1) get/set |
636	| `dict[DiscountType, DiscountStrategy]` | strategy registry | enum → strategy in O(1); the alternative is an `if/elif` ladder in `redeem` that grows with every kind | O(1) |
637	| linked `ValidationHandler` chain | rule pipeline | order is policy and rules are independent; alternative was a hardcoded sequence of `if`s — couples order to `redeem` and isn't reorderable per market | O(r), r = #rules (4) |
638	| `dict[str, int]` `_per_user_counts` | per-user tally on the coupon | O(1) lookup by user; the alternative (a separate `dict[(code,user)] -> int` on the service) splits the count from the coupon and loses the natural per-coupon lock granularity | O(1) |
639	| `dict[str, RLock]` `_locks` (thread-safe only) | one lock per coupon | per-coupon locks let different coupons redeem in parallel; one global lock serializes every coupon through one bottleneck | O(1) get-or-create |
640	
641	The chain over a hardcoded `if`-ladder is the judgment call: at 4 rules an `if`-ladder is fine, but rule *order* is a per-market policy here, and the chain makes that order injectable data — worth the one extra interface.
642	
643	### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix
644	
645	> **🧠 Claim:** The global-limit re-check must happen INSIDE the lock, not just in the chain.
646	> **🔬 Proof:** `GlobalLimitHandler` runs outside the lock. Two threads both read `remaining_uses=1`, both pass the chain, both enter `redeem`. If the spend just incremented without re-checking, both would push `redeemed_count` past `usage_limit`.
647	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Coupon.try_consume` re-checks `remaining_uses <= 0` first thing, and the thread-safe service wraps the call in a per-coupon lock — so the re-check + increment are one atomic step.
648	
649	> **🧠 Claim:** `remaining_uses` must be DERIVED, never stored.
650	> **🔬 Proof:** If we stored a separate `remaining` field and bumped it independently of `redeemed_count`, a missed update in one path leaves the two disagreeing — and "how many are left" becomes ambiguous.
651	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `remaining_uses` is a `@property` returning `usage_limit - redeemed_count`. One source of truth.
652	
653	> **🧠 Claim:** A coupon used at the EXACT expiry instant is valid; only strictly-past is rejected.
654	> **🔬 Proof:** Using `now >= expires_at` would reject a redemption that lands on the very millisecond of expiry, which is surprising and off-by-one.
655	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ExpiryHandler` uses `if now > coupon.expires_at`. The test `test_coupon_valid_exactly_at_expiry_instant` pins this boundary.
656	
657	> **🧠 Claim:** Money must be integer cents with floor division, never floats.
658	> **🔬 Proof:** `0.20 * 2999` in floats gives `599.8` and float drift; a percent coupon could compute a discount a cent off and fail reconciliation.
659	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `PercentOffStrategy` does `subtotal * value // 100` (integer floor), clamped to subtotal. Flat and BOGO also clamp to subtotal so `final >= 0`.
660	
661	> **🧠 Claim:** Locking the dict-of-locks itself matters in the thread-safe version.
662	> **🔬 Proof:** Two threads redeeming the SAME coupon for the first time both call `_locks[code] = RLock()` — without guarding the dict, that create-on-miss can race and they end up with two different lock objects, so the per-coupon lock guards nothing.
663	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `with self._map_lock: lock = self._locks.setdefault(code, RLock())` — the map lock makes the get-or-create atomic, then we lock the coupon's `RLock`.
664	
665	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
666	
667	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
668	> ```
669	> def redeem(self, code, cart):
670	>     coupon = self._coupons[code]
671	>     if coupon.remaining_uses <= 0:           # check
672	>         raise LimitExhausted(...)
673	>     discount = self._discount_for(coupon, cart)
674	>     coupon.redeemed_count += 1               # act — two lines later!
675	>     return Redemption(...)
676	> ```
677	> **Why it's wrong:** the check and the act are separated, so under concurrency many threads pass the check before any increment lands — classic check-then-act over-spend. It also bypasses per-user limits and bumps the counter by hand, scattering the "never below zero" rule. The correct version delegates to `Coupon.try_consume` (check + decrement as one method on the owner) and lifts that one call under a per-coupon lock in §9.
